text: Regulation B requires that a creditor evaluate each applicant's creditworthiness on an individual basis. A creditor may not use a credit scoring system that is not empirically derived and statistically sound.

The Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act requires financial institutions to provide a privacy notice to customers at account opening and annually thereafter. The notice must describe the institution's information sharing practices.

Under the Community Reinvestment Act, institutions must document their efforts to meet the credit needs of all segments of their communities including low and moderate income areas. CRA ratings are considered in merger and acquisition approvals.

Automated underwriting systems must be validated for disparate impact annually. Any model that produces statistically significant disparate impact on a protected class must be reviewed and justified or replaced.

The Home Mortgage Disclosure Act requires lenders to collect and report data on mortgage applications including the race, ethnicity, and sex of applicants. HMDA data is used by regulators to identify potential fair lending violations.

Loan pricing must be risk-based and documented. Exceptions to standard pricing must be approved by a credit officer and logged in the exception tracking system. Exception rates above 10 percent trigger a fair lending review.

When an adverse action is based on a credit score, the creditor must disclose the score used, the range of possible scores, the key factors that adversely affected the score, and the date the score was created.

Counter-offers that differ materially from the terms requested must be treated as adverse action if the applicant does not accept the counter-offer. The creditor must provide an adverse action notice within 90 days.

Adverse action notices must be retained for 25 months for consumer credit applications. The retention period begins on the date the notice was sent. Records must be retrievable within 5 business days of a regulatory request.

Chargeback rights under Regulation E apply to electronic fund transfers including ACH debits, debit card transactions, and peer-to-peer payments. The consumer must initiate a dispute within 60 days of the statement date.

When a dispute investigation results in a finding in favor of the consumer, any provisional credit previously issued becomes permanent. If the investigation finds in favor of the merchant, the provisional credit must be reversed with 5 days notice.

Disputes involving point-of-sale transactions at merchant locations require the institution to obtain transaction documentation from the merchant's acquiring bank. The acquiring bank has 20 business days to respond to a retrieval request.

Identity theft victims may place an initial fraud alert lasting one year or an extended alert lasting seven years. During an extended alert, the creditor must use reasonable policies to verify the consumer's identity before extending credit.

Financial institutions must implement a written Identity Theft Prevention Program under the Red Flags Rule. The program must identify relevant red flags, detect red flags, respond appropriately, and be updated periodically.

Currency Transaction Reports must be filed for cash transactions exceeding $10,000 in a single business day. Structuring transactions to avoid the CTR threshold is a federal crime under the Bank Secrecy Act.

Debt collectors must cease communication with a consumer upon receipt of a written cease communication request. The only permitted subsequent contact is to notify the consumer of specific remedies the collector intends to invoke.

Skip tracing activities must comply with the FDCPA prohibition on false representations. Collectors may contact third parties only to locate the consumer and may not reveal that the consumer owes a debt.
Payment arrangements entered into during collections must be documented with the agreed amount, frequency, and duration. Broken arrangements trigger an escalation review within 3 business days.

Prior to charge-off, the account must be reviewed for bankruptcy status. If a bankruptcy petition is filed, the automatic stay prohibits further collection activity and the account must be flagged in the servicing system within 24 hours.

Deficiency balances remaining after collateral liquidation must be pursued within the statute of limitations of the applicable state. The statute of limitations clock restarts upon any written acknowledgment of the debt by the consumer.

When selling charged-off debt, the seller must warrant that the accounts are not subject to active bankruptcy proceedings, active fraud investigations, or pending litigation. Breach of warranty entitles the buyer to return the account.

The CARD Act prohibits over-limit fees unless the cardholder has opted in to over-limit coverage. Opt-in must be obtained separately from other account agreements and may be revoked by the cardholder at any time.

Rate increases on existing balances are prohibited under the CARD Act except in specific circumstances including a promotional rate expiration, a variable rate index change, or failure to make a minimum payment within 60 days.

Annual fee disclosures must be provided at least 45 days before the fee is charged. If the cardholder does not accept the fee, they must be permitted to close the account and pay off the balance under the existing terms.

Escrow accounts for mortgage loans must be analyzed annually. Any shortage must be collected over a 12-month period. Surpluses exceeding $50 must be refunded to the borrower within 30 days of the annual analysis.

Loss mitigation applications for mortgage loans must be acknowledged within 5 business days. The servicer must evaluate all loss mitigation options for which the borrower may be eligible before referring the loan to foreclosure.

The Equal Credit Opportunity Act prohibits discrimination in any aspect of a credit transaction. This includes advertising, application procedures, credit evaluation, and account servicing. Violations may result in actual and punitive damages.

Flood insurance must be required for loans secured by improved real property located in a Special Flood Hazard Area. The institution must notify the borrower of the flood insurance requirement at least 10 days before loan closing.

Appraisals for higher-priced mortgage loans must be conducted by a certified or licensed appraiser. A second appraisal is required if the seller acquired the property within 180 days at a lower price.

Ability-to-repay requirements under Regulation Z require creditors to make a reasonable good faith determination that the consumer has the ability to repay the loan. Qualified Mortgage status provides a safe harbor for this determination.
